label: Zócalo / Plaza de la Constitución
sub: Mexico City's main square and political heart. Site of Aztec Templo Mayor, viceregal palace, and today's Palacio Nacional. Hosts state ceremonies, political protests, and millions of annual visitors.

label: Palacio Nacional
sub: Federal executive headquarters built on the ruins of Moctezuma II's palace. Houses Diego Rivera's famous murals. Seat of the Mexican presidency. The Grito de Independencia bell is rung from its balcony on September 15.

label: Templo Mayor
sub: Main temple of Tenochtitlan, rediscovered beneath Mexico City's historic center in 1978. The two-shrine pyramid was dedicated to Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc. Now an INAH archaeological zone and museum with 8,000+ recovered artifacts.

label: Palacio de Bellas Artes
sub: Mexico's premier cultural venue. Begun under Porfirio Díaz (1904), completed 1934. Famous for its sinking foundation (visibly tilting due to subsidence) and interior murals by Rivera, Orozco, Siqueiros, and Tamayo.

label: Ángel de la Independencia
sub: Mexico City's iconic golden-winged column on Paseo de la Reforma. Inaugurated 1910 by Porfirio Díaz. De facto gathering point for national celebrations (football, elections) and mass protests. Damaged by 1957 and 1985 earthquakes.

label: Plaza de las Tres Culturas (Tlatelolco)
sub: Site of the October 2, 1968 Tlatelolco Massacre, where government forces killed student protesters 10 days before the Mexico City Olympics. 'Three Cultures' refers to Aztec ruins, colonial church, and 1960s apartment blocks. Annual memorial held each October 2.

label: Basílica de Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe
sub: Most-visited Catholic pilgrimage site in the world, with 20+ million annual visitors. Built at Tepeyac Hill where Juan Diego reported the 1531 Marian apparition. The modern tilma-housing basilica (inaugurated 1976) replaced the sinking 18th-century structure.

label: Bosque de Chapultepec
sub: One of the largest urban parks in the world (686 ha). Houses Chapultepec Castle (former imperial palace, now national history museum), the National Museum of Anthropology, the National Auditorium, and Mexico City's zoo. Sacred to the Mexica before the Conquest.

label: Ciudad Universitaria — UNAM
sub: UNESCO World Heritage Site (2007). Mexico's largest university campus, inaugurated 1952. Features murals by Diego Rivera and Juan O'Gorman. 350,000+ students. Epicenter of the 1968 student movement that preceded Tlatelolco.

label: Estadio Azteca
sub: World's only stadium to host two FIFA World Cup finals (1970, 1986). Capacity ~87,000. Site of Pelé's 1970 World Cup victory and Maradona's 'Hand of God' goal (1986). Underwent major renovations for the 2026 FIFA World Cup (match venue).

label: Xochimilco — Aztec Chinampas
sub: UNESCO World Heritage Site (1987). The last remaining Aztec chinampa (floating garden) system in Mexico City. Xochimilco's canals and trajineras (colorful boats) date to pre-Hispanic times. Ecological reserve increasingly threatened by urban growth and water scarcity.

label: AICM — Aeropuerto Internacional Benito Juárez
sub: Mexico City's main airport, handling ~40 million passengers/year. Built on the dried lakebed of Lake Texcoco, it is sinking ~30cm/year due to subsidence. AMLO cancelled the replacement NAICM project (2018) by popular referendum; capacity pressure continues.

label: Metro Line 12 Collapse Site (Olivos–Nopalera)
sub: Site where an elevated guideway section of Line 12 collapsed on May 3, 2021, killing 26 and injuring 98. A forensic investigation by DNV found design flaws and deficient bolt installation from the original 2012 construction. The rebuilt section reopened January 2023.

label: Metro Pantitlán — Largest Transfer Hub
sub: Mexico City's busiest Metro station and the largest transit hub in Latin America, serving Lines 1, 5, 9, and A. Handles ~450,000 passengers daily. Also connects to bus terminals serving the eastern metropolitan area. Subject to chronic overcrowding and security concerns.

label: Secretaría de Seguridad Ciudadana (SSC)
sub: Mexico City's citizen security ministry, responsible for ~20,000 police officers. Under Omar García Harfuch (2019-2024) and now Pablo Vázquez Camacho, it implemented C5 surveillance, neighborhood policing, and femicide protocols. Homicide rate fell from ~17/100k (2018) to ~7.8/100k (2024).

label: Monumento a la Revolución
sub: The world's largest monument to a revolution (inaugurated 1938). Originally designed as a legislative palace under Porfirio Díaz but converted into a monument after the Revolution. Houses the tombs of Pancho Villa, Plutarco Elías Calles, Lázaro Cárdenas, Francisco I. Madero, and Venustiano Carranza.

label: Estadio Olímpico Universitario (1968)
sub: Host stadium of the 1968 Summer Olympics athletics events. The volcano-shaped stadium (designed by Augusto Pérez Palacios) features a Diego Rivera bas-relief at its entrance. Today serves as the UNAM Pumas football club home ground. Capacity: 72,000.

label: SACMEX — Water System Distribution Hub
sub: Sistema de Aguas de la Ciudad de México (SACMEX) manages CDMX's water infrastructure: 13,000+ km of pipes, 11 water treatment plants, and the Cutzamala distribution system. The city loses ~40% of water to leaks. Ground subsidence at 30–50cm/yr in many districts undermines pipe integrity.

label: Plaza Garibaldi
sub: The spiritual home of mariachi music in Mexico. Since the 1920s, hundreds of mariachi musicians have gathered here nightly to serenade patrons. Adjacent to the Museo del Tequila y Mezcal. The square has undergone security upgrades following periodic violence in the surrounding area.

label: Paseo de la Reforma — Financial Axis
sub: Mexico City's grand boulevard, commissioned by Emperor Maximilian I (1864) as Paseo del Emperador. Home to Torre BBVA (209m), Torre Mayor (225m), the Stock Exchange (BMV), major embassies, luxury hotels, and the Ángel monument. Scene of feminist marches, pro-democracy demonstrations, and Champions League celebrations.

label: Tepito — 'Barrio Bravo'
sub: One of CDMX's most notorious neighborhoods, historically controlled by La Unión Tepito (emerged ~2009), which dominates extortion, drug trafficking, and piracy markets. Despite major police operations under García Harfuch, Tepito remains a flashpoint for cartel violence. Also home to Mexico's largest Santa Muerte shrine and a resilient working-class culture.

label: Colonia Roma Norte — Gentrification Ground Zero
sub: Once a working-class neighborhood, Roma Norte became the epicenter of CDMX's affordability crisis after the 2017 earthquake and the COVID-era digital nomad boom. Rents across the city rose ~94% in this period. Alfonso Cuarón's film Roma (2018) brought global attention to the area. Anti-displacement marches and calls to limit Airbnb and foreign remote workers have repeatedly centered here.

label: Iztapalapa — Most Populous Alcaldía
sub: Home to 1.835 million residents (2020 census), Iztapalapa is CDMX's most populous borough. Under Clara Brugada (2018–2022), homicides fell 60% and it dropped from 8th to 36th in the national dangerous municipalities ranking — a flagship example of social-investment-based security. Site of the famous Semana Santa Via Crucis Passion play, drawing 150,000+ on Good Friday.

label: Suprema Corte de Justicia de la Nación (SCJN)
sub: Mexico's highest court, located steps from the Zócalo. The court became a constitutional battleground in 2024: MORENA's Congress passed a judicial reform (published September 15, 2024) requiring judges to be popularly elected. Eight of eleven SCJN justices resigned in protest. The U.S., Canada, and EU condemned the reform as a threat to judicial independence and USMCA obligations.

label: Palacio de Lecumberri — Archivo General de la Nación
sub: Built as Mexico's model panopticon prison (opened 1900 under Porfirio Díaz), 'El Palacio Negro' held political prisoners including Pancho Villa (escaped 1912), David Alfaro Siqueiros, José Revueltas, and hundreds arrested after the 1968 Tlatelolco Massacre. Converted on August 27, 1982 into the Archivo General de la Nación (national archives). Its star-shaped panopticon is a Porfirian architectural landmark.
